With  the  rapid advancement  of deep neural  networks
(DNNs),  artificial  intelligence has  achieved significant
progress in fields such as medical diagnostics [35], image
classification [19, 23], and autonomous driving [12, 27].

Figure 1. Comparison of multi-target approaches. For target label
processing, previous methods (top row) typically encode labels as
one-dimensional tensors (e.g., one-hot or CLIP embeddings) to
guide noise generation. However, such one-dimensional guidance
often leads to loss of fine-grained details since images are inher-
ently two-dimensional, which may cause overfitting to the features
emphasized by the surrogate model during training.  In contrast
(bottom row), our method generates two-dimensional latent repre-
sentations from target labels, which better preserves structural in-
formation when guiding the noise generation process. AE stands
for adversarial example.
However,  the adversarial vulnerability of deep learning
models has emerged as a major challenge affecting their re-
liable application in security-sensitive scenarios.
Adversarial attacks manipulate input data by introducing
subtle and imperceptible perturbations, misleading model
predictions, and exposing the fragility of DNNs. These at-
tacks can be categorized into two main types: untargeted at-
tacks, which aim to cause misclassification without specify-
ing a particular category, and targeted attacks, which force
models to output labels chosen by the attacker.  Targeted
attacks are not only more challenging but also more threat-
ening as they disrupt model reliability and enable specific
malicious control, such as deceiving an autonomous vehi-
cle into interpreting a stop sign as a speed limit sign.
In real-world applications,  attackers  typically do not

semantic details (e.g., the “pointer” of a barometer) and the target sem
fully understand the architecture or parameters of the target
model, making transferability a crucial property for effec-
tive adversarial attacks.  Transferability refers to the abil-
ity of adversarial examples crafted on surrogate models to
successfully deceive unknown black-box models [50, 54].
While untargeted attacks have demonstrated strong trans-
ferability, targeted attacks still suffer from low success rates
due to their dependency on overfitting the decision bound-
aries of surrogate models.
Existing targeted attack methods primarily fall into two
categories:  instance-specific approaches [9, 15, 29] and
instance-agnostic approaches [14, 27, 34]. Instance-specific
methods [9, 10, 54] optimize adversarial perturbations for
individual samples in an iterative manner, often leading to
inefficiency and susceptibility to overfitting. On the other
hand, instance-agnostic approaches focus on learning uni-
versal perturbations [28, 37, 58] or generators [39, 41]
based on data distribution to enhance attack generality.
Recent generative techniques,  such as C-GSP [56] and
CGNC [13], have significantly improved efficiency by train-
ing conditional generators for multiple target classes com-
pared to single-target methods.  However,  these methods
fail to thoroughly investigate the key factors affecting attack
transferability.
To address this gap, we observe that adversarial noise
generated for targeted attacks functions similarly to “im-
planting” semantic features of the target class into source

ods (CGSP, CGNC, and our proposed method (2D-TGAF)) for
rial attacks are performed with two distinct target labels: “barometer”

antic quantity (e.g., the number of “figs”).
images.  Building on this insight, we define two critical
factors that influence attack transferability: the quality and
quantity of target-class semantic features in the generated
noise. Through experimental analysis (Figure 3), we vali-
date our hypothesis.

Furthermore, we examine the adversar-
ial noise produced by C-GSP and CGNC and identify issues
related to incomplete semantic feature implantation and in-
sufficient semantic representation, as shown in Figure 2.
We hypothesize that these shortcomings arise because these
methods represent target labels as one-dimensional tensors
(e.g., one-hot encoding or CLIP embeddings), disregard-
ing spatial and structural information, and tend to overfit
to the regions where noise mapping is easiest during train-
ing. Consequently, the generated adversarial noise lacks es-
sential target-class features and sufficient semantic informa-
tion, thereby limiting its transferability.
To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel gen-
erative framework termed 2D-Tensor-Guided Adversarial
Fusion (2D-TGAF). The core innovation of our approach
lies in leveraging 2D spatial information of target labels to
guide adversarial noise generation. This enables the reten-
tion of fixed low-level semantic information during the gen-
eration process, rather than relying solely on the decision
boundaries of surrogate models. Additionally, we introduce
a carefully designed random masking strategy tailored for
training, ensuring that parts of the generated noise still con-
tain complete semantic information of the target class. Our.

2.1. Transferable Targeted Attacks
Transferable targeted adversarial attacks aim to generate
adversarial examples capable of reliably deceiving multi-
ple models into misclassifying them into a specific target
class.  These methods can be broadly classified into four
categories:  input transformation-based methods, advanced
objective-based approaches, generation-based methods, and
ensemble-based techniques.  Input  transformation-based
methods  [1, 33, 51] improve attack transferability by di-
versifying input  representations through techniques such
as geometric  transformations  and  local mixup.  Ad-
vanced objective-based approaches [2, 26, 52, 57, 60] re-
fine loss functions or optimize specific outputs,  like log-
its,  to enhance the precision and effectiveness of the at-
tack. Generation-based methods [38, 59] employ generative
models to create adversarial examples that closely resemble

the characteristics of the target class. Ensemble-based tech-
niques [53] focus on efficiently integrating multiple models
or strategies to enhance the robustness and generalization of
adversarial examples across varying architectures, leverag-
ing approaches like self-distillation and weight scaling.
2.2. Diffusion Models for Adversarial Attack
Recent research [3–5] has demonstrated that Denoising Dif-
fusion Probabilistic Model  (DDPM) [22] and Denoising
Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) [44], originally designed
for high-quality image generation, can be repurposed for
crafting adversarial examples with enhanced stealth and
transferability. The core idea leverages the models’ denois-
ing process: for a noisy input xt at step t ∈ {1, . . . , T}, a
trained model ϵθ iteratively predicts and removes noise to
gradually recover the clean data. While DDPM follows a
Markovian diffusion process, DDIM generalizes it with a
non-Markovian approach.
The forward corruption process is defined as:
(cid:16)  (cid:112)  (cid:17)
q(xt | xt−1) = N xt;  1 − βt xt−1, βt I  ,  (1)
in which the original data is gradually corrupted by adding
Gaussian noise. Conversely, the reverse generation process
for DDPM is defined as:
pθ(xt−1 | xt) = N (xt−1; µθ(xt, t), Σθ(xt, t)) .  (2)
Through this process,  the model learns to recover an es-
timate of the clean image at each timestep based on the
noisy input. DDIM reparameterizes this reverse process to
directly map noise predictions to the clean data.
The interplay between the forward corruption and re-
verse generation processes enables diffusion models to cap-
ture the natural  image manifold.  For example, DiffAt-
tack [3] leverages the latent space of pretrained diffusion
models to optimize adversarial perturbations, while Ad-
vDiff [5] introduces two novel adversarial guidance strate-
gies during the reverse generation process to produce un-
restricted adversarial examples. These approaches vividly
demonstrate the potential and flexibility of diffusion mod-
els in the field of adversarial example generation.
2.3. Denfensive Models
To mitigate  adversarial  attacks,  various  defense  ap-
proaches have been proposed, including adversarial train-
ing (AT), preprocessing-based methods,  and feature de-
noising. Among these, AT [17, 36, 48] stands out as one
of the most effective techniques, improving model robust-
ness by incorporating adversarial examples during train-
ing.  Preprocessing-based defenses [7, 11, 55],  on the
other hand,  remove adversarial perturbations from inputs
before inference.  For example, BitSqueeze [55] reduces
adversarial effects through bit-depth compression, while

In this section, we first introduce the prerequisite knowledge
and our motivation. Then, we provide a detailed explana-
tion of our 2D-TGAF method. To highlight the differences
between our approach and other methods, we also summa-
rize the distinctions between our method and CGNC.
3.1. Preliminaries
Let fΦ : X → Y denote a white-box image classifier pa-

scores over L classes. Given a natural image x ∈ X and a
target class ct ∈ C, the goal of transferable targeted adver-
sarial attacks is to craft an imperceptible perturbation δ such
that the adversarial example xadv = x+δ misleads both the
surrogate model fθ and unknown black-box victim models
FΦ into predicting ct. This objective can be formalized as
follows:

To achieve this objective, numerous instance-specific
methods have been proposed. However, these approaches
often require a large number of iterations per attack, result-
ing in high computational cost and low efficiency, which
renders them impractical for real-world applications. To ad-
dress this limitation, several multi-target methods have been
developed, primarily based on a generative framework.
Existing generative approaches train a conditional per-
turbation generator Gθ to map input pairs (x, ct) to targeted
perturbations, i.e., δ = Gθ(x, ct). The optimization objec-
tive is formulated as:

Here, X represents an unlabeled training set and L(·, ·) is
typically the cross-entropy loss.
In the CGSP [56] method, the target label is encoded as a
one-hot vector to guide the generation process; however, the
discrete nature of this encoding limits its ability to leverage
rich semantic priors of target classes. To overcome this lim-
itation, CGNC [13] employs CLIP to continuously encode
the target label, thereby enhancing performance.

3.2. Motivation
Although recent advances in targeted adversarial attacks
have been made, the transferability of generated adversarial
samples remains unsatisfactory. In non-targeted adversarial
attacks, numerous studies [32, 49, 61] have highlighted that
different models focusing on distinct object regions impede
the generalization of adversarial samples. This observation
naturally leads us to investigate:
What primarily hinders the generalizability of adversar-
ial samples in targeted attacks?
Existing studies [13, 56] suggest  that  targeted attacks
achieve their objective by “implanting” target-class seman-
tic features into source images. Based on this, we hypoth-
esize that the nature of these implanted features is the key
factor influencing attack success. Specifically, we examine:
1) Feature quality: The integrity and detail preservation of
implanted features.  2) Feature quantity: The number of
target-class features introduced into the source image. As
shown in Figure 3, our experiments reveal two critical phe-
nomena: 1) When essential details (e.g., buttons on a trench
coat) are masked, models exhibit high-confidence misclas-
sifications (e.g., predicting “lab coat” instead of the true
class).  2) Increasing the number of implanted target fea-
tures (e.g., sea lion patches) raises the likelihood of target-
class predictions. These two factors synergistically impact
the generalizability of adversarial samples.  Since differ-
ent models focus on disparate object regions, feature omis-
sions may precisely prevent certain models from capturing
their critical discriminative features, while insufficient fea-
ture quantity further constrains attack effectiveness.
Based on our previous findings, we analyzed the adver-
sarial noise generated by different multi-target attack ap-
proaches. As shown in Figure 2, we found that: 1) for the
barometer class, the adversarial noise produced by CGSP
and CGNC methods lacks essential structural features in-
cluding the pointer and scale markings; 2) for the fig class,
these methods generate noise that primarily concentrates at
image boundaries while exhibiting overfitting due to insuffi-
cient feature diversity. These findings prompt a novel ques-
tion:
How can we create adversarial noise that incorporates
a greater quantity and higher completeness of target fea-
tures?
To address this issue, we first conducted a analysis of ex-
isting approaches. As illustrated in Figure 1, current meth-
ods universally encode target labels as one-dimensional ten-
sors for processing. However, this one-dimensional encod-
ing scheme fundamentally differs from the two-dimensional
nature of images, resulting in the loss of low-level visual in-
formation.

Such information deficiency introduces bias dur-
ing the training of multi-target attack models:  the models
tend to learn specific feature representations from surrogate
models, consequently leading to overfitting.

Figure 4. The framework of 2D Target-Guided Adversarial Fusio
(c) Convolution-based Fusion (CbF) module; (d) Transformer-based F
OIE (Original Image Embeddings), FE (Fusion Embeddings), TIE (T
Regarding the issue of insufficient feature injection, prior
research [25] has demonstrated that deep neural networks
inherently adhere to the principle of Occam’s razor. Build-
ing upon this finding, we propose a feature enhancement ap-
proach to improve performance by increasing the number of
effective features in perturbation. Specifically, inspired by
the fine-tuning method in CGNC [13], we designed an in-
novative masked training mechanism tailored for training.
This mechanism employs random masking on the model’s
output noise during training, ensuring that partial noise re-
gions retain complete feature information.
3.3. 2D Target-Guided Adversarial Fusion
The architecture of our proposed 2D-TGAF method is illus-
trated in Figure 4. Specifically, 2D-TGAF comprises four
key components: an Image Encoder E, a Text-to-Image En-
coder G, a Feature Integration Module F, and an Image De-
coder D. We next detail the design of each component.
Image Encoder module.  Given an input image I ∈
RC×H×W
,  our encoder E extracts multi-scale features
through three convolutional  layers, gradually abstracting
and downsampling the features. Specifically, we transform

employing the encoder and denoising Unet parts of Stable-
Diffusion-2 [42] to process the input target labels, resulting
in low-dimensional latent vectors of size B × 4 × 64 × 64.

1.  Convolution-based Fusion (CbF): We employ a

interactions.  The 1 × 1 convolution allows the model to
capture channel-wise relationships and compress the feature
space.

4.1. Experimental Settings
Datasets.  Following prior works  [14, 56], the generator
is trained on the ImageNet training set  [6], while the ad-
versarial attack performance is evaluated on the ImageNet-
NeurIPS (1k) dataset  [40].

posed 2D-TGAF method across three types of settings. 1)
Normally trained models, including Inception-v3 (Inc-v3)
[46],  Inception-v4 (Inc-v4)  [47],  Inception-ResNet-v2

Robustly trained models, which consist of adv-Inception-

Defense methods, consisting of two main strategies: one
based on preprocessing techniques, such as JPEG compres-
sion [11], BitSqueezing [55], and Smoothing [7], and the
other focused on denoising, such as the high-level represen-
tation guided denoiser (HGD)  [30].

Implementation Details.  Following previous works
[14, 56], we use Inception-v3 (Inc-v3) and ResNet-152
(Res-152) as surrogate models for training the generator.
The perturbation budget is set to ϵ = 16/255.  The gen-
erator is trained for 10 epochs with a learning rate of 2e-4
and a batch size of 16. The number of blocks, N, is set to 3.
4.2. Evaluation on Normal Models
We first assess the transferability of adversarial examples
generated by normally trained models. The results are sum-
marized in Table 1, where the first column represents the
surrogate model, the second column lists the attack meth-
ods, and the remaining columns show the Attack Success
Rate (ASR) of various classification models under attack.
The reported results are averaged over 8 different  target
classes.
Our proposed method, 2D-TGAF, consistently achieves
state-of-the-art  (SOTA) performance,  demonstrating the
highest average ASR across nearly all experimental con-
figurations.  Specifically, when using Inc-v3 and Res-152
as surrogate models, our approach achieves remarkable im-
provements in black-box ASR, outperforming the previous
SOTA method CGNC by 16.11% and 5.89%, respectively.

Furthermore, while enhancing the transferability of black-
box scenarios, our method also improves white-box attack
performance, achieving ASRs of 98.15% for Inc-v3 and
97.79% for Res-152, respectively.
4.3. Evaluation on Robust Models
Building upon the findings obtained with normally trained
models, we proceed to evaluate the performance of our
method on robustly trained models.  The summarized re-
sults are presented in Table 2.
From these results,  it  is evident  that our method also
achieves SOTA performance in ASR across most experi-
mental settings. Specifically, when using Inc-v3 and Res-
152 as surrogate models, our approach delivers average
ASR improvements of 16.92% and 5.66% over CGNC, and
exceeds other methods by more than 30% and 10%, respec-
tively. This notable improvement highlights the effective-
ness of our method in bypassing the defenses of adversari-
ally robust networks.
4.4. Evaluation on Defense Methods
To further evaluate the robustness of our proposed 2D-
TGAF, we evaluate 2D-TGAF against multiple defense
mechanisms (JPEG compression, BitSqueezing, Smooth-
ing, and HGD) and compare it with CGNC.

Based on the results in Table 3, 2D-TGAF consistently
outperforms CGNC across all tested defense mechanisms
when attacking the VGG-16 target model. Under smooth-
ing defenses, 2D-TGAF achieves significantly higher at-
tack ASR, particularly with Medium smoothing (45.23%
vs. 30.21% for Inc-v3) and maintains an advantage in Gaus-
sian smoothing and Average smoothing.  Similarly, under
BitSqueezing, 2D-TGAF demonstrates superior robustness,
especially at 5-bit compression (68.36% vs.  50.10% for
Inc-v3). The trend continues with HGD, where 2D-TGAF
achieves 83.02% ASR for Res-152,  surpassing CGNC’s
78.91%. However, it is observed that under stricter prepro-
cessing parameters, the absolute values of ASR and the per-
formance gap between 2D-TGAF and CGNC narrow. This
is likely due to the stricter preprocessing significantly de-
grading the image information, thereby reducing the attack
effectiveness of both methods and limiting the potential for
differentiation between them.  These results confirm that
2D-TGAF is more resilient against preprocessing-based de-
fenses and denoising techniques.

influencing the transferability of adversarial attacks.  To
address these challenges, we propose 2D-TGAF, a novel
method that leverages the powerful generative abilities of
diffusion models to encode target labels as 2D tensors. This
finer-grained approach helps guide noise generation,  im-
proving the quality of target semantic information. Further-
more, we introduce a masking strategy during the training
phase to ensure that the portion of the generated noise re-
tains complete target semantic features, thus enhancing the
quantity. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that 2D-
TGAF not only surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods
on normally trained models but also shows robust perfor-
mance against a variety of defense strategies.  This high-
lights its superior effectiveness in ensuring high attack suc-
cess rates and transferability. We hope that our proposed
method serves as a reliable tool for evaluating model ro-
bustness under black-box attack settings and provides new
insight for further research on vulnerability and robustness
in adversarial scenarios.
